content:
Invisible, but everywhere

3 min read
4 hours ago

--

A few years ago, WebAssembly (WASM) was surrounded by enormous expectations. Depending on who you listened to, it would either revolutionize software delivery or completely replace JavaScript. Today, many developers ask a different question: What happened to WebAssembly?
The interesting answer is that WebAssembly did not disappear at all. In fact, it quietly became part of the infrastructure powering many modern applications — just often behind the scenes.
Tools like Godot use WebAssembly to export games directly to the browser. Figma relies on it to run performance-critical C++ code on the web. Ruffle revives old Flash content safely inside modern browsers using WASM, while browser-based development platforms like StackBlitz use it to power web containers. Even image-processing tools such as Squoosh.app depend heavily on WebAssembly under the hood.
The reason this progress feels less visible is that WebAssembly evolved mostly as an infrastructure technology, not as a direct replacement for the web itself. JavaScript still handles interfaces and browser interaction, while WASM increasingly acts as a high-performance compute layer. In many ways, this is exactly the role where it shines best.
That division is becoming especially important for scientific computing, media processing, simulations, visualization, and browser-based tools that benefit from native-level performance without requiring installation…
